concepts.name: generalization error
concepts.description:
Test error, also referred to as generalization error, is the prediction error
over an independent test sample

concepts.name: deviance
concepts.description:
The quantity −2 × the log-likelihood is sometimes referred to as the
deviance.
Again, test error here is ErrT = E[L(G, ˆG(X))|T ], the population mis-
classiﬁcation error of the classiﬁer trained on T , and Err is the expected
misclassiﬁcation error.
Training error is the sample analogue, for example,

concepts.name: Bayes factor
concepts.description:
is called the Bayes factor, the contribution of the data toward the posterior
odds.
Typically we assume that the prior over models is uniform, so that
Pr(Mm) is constant. We need some way of approximating Pr(Z|Mm).
A so-called Laplace approximation to the integral followed by some other
simpliﬁcations (Ripley, 1996, page 64) to (7.37) gives

concepts.name: leave-one-out cross-validation
concepts.description:
Typical choices of K are 5 or 10 (see below). The case K = N is known
as leave-one-out cross-validation. In this case κ(i) = i, and for the ith
observation the ﬁt is computed using all the data except the ith.
Given a set of models f(x, α) indexed by a tuning parameter α, denote
by ˆf −k(x, α) the αth model ﬁt with the kth part of the data removed. Then
for this set of models we deﬁne

concepts.name: leave-one-out
concepts.description:
Figures 7.14 and 7.15 examine the question of whether cross-validation does
a good job in estimating ErrT , the error conditional on a given training set
T (expression (7.15) on page 228), as opposed to the expected test error.
For each of 100 training sets generated from the “reg/linear” setting in
the top-right panel of Figure 7.3, Figure 7.14 shows the conditional error
curves ErrT as a function of subset size (top left). The next two panels show
10-fold and N-fold cross-validation, the latter also known as leave-one-out
(LOO). The thick red curve in each plot is the expected error Err, while
the thick black curves are the expected cross-validation curves. The lower
right panel shows how well cross-validation approximates the conditional
and expected error.
One might have expected N-fold CV to approximate ErrT well, since it
almost uses the full training sample to ﬁt a new test point. 10-fold CV, on
the other hand, might be expected to estimate Err well, since it averages
over somewhat diﬀerent training sets. From the ﬁgure it appears 10-fold
does a better job than N-fold in estimating ErrT , and estimates Err even
better. Indeed, the similarity of the two black curves with the red curve
suggests both CV curves are approximately unbiased for Err, with 10-fold
having less variance. Similar trends were reported by Efron (1983).
Figure 7.15 shows scatterplots of both 10-fold and N-fold cross-validation
error estimates versus the true conditional error for the 100 simulations.
Although the scatterplots do not indicate much correlation, the lower right
panel shows that for the most part the correlations are negative, a curi-
ous phenomenon that has been observed before. This negative correlation
explains why neither form of CV estimates ErrT well. The broken lines in
each plot are drawn at Err(p), the expected error for the best subset of
size p. We see again that both forms of CV are approximately unbiased for
expected error, but the variation in test error for diﬀerent training sets is
quite substantial.
Among the four experimental conditions in 7.3, this “reg/linear” scenario
showed the highest correlation between actual and predicted test error. This

concepts.name: test error
concepts.description: Prediction error over an independent test sample drawn from the same population as the training data.

concepts.name: generalized cross-validation
concepts.description: A convenient approximation to leave-one-out cross-validation for linear fitting under squared-error loss, using GCV = (1/N)Σᵢ[yᵢ - f̂(xᵢ)]²/(1 - trace(S)/N)² instead of computing individual diagonal elements Sᵢᵢ.

concepts.name: estimation bias
concepts.description: The error between the average estimate and the best-fitting model within a class. Zero for ordinary least squares but positive for restricted fits like ridge regression.

concepts.name: akaike information criterion
concepts.description: A general estimate of expected prediction error for log-likelihood loss, using the formula -2·loglik + 2·d, relying on the asymptotic relationship ω = (2/N)Σᵢ Cov(ŷᵢ, yᵢ).

concepts.name: bayesian information criterion
concepts.description: A model selection criterion with the form -2·loglik + log(N)·d, also known as the Schwarz criterion. Motivated by Bayesian posterior probability and equivalent to choosing the model with largest approximate posterior probability.

concepts.name: laplace approximation
concepts.description: An approximation technique used to approximate the integral Pr(Z|Mₘ) in Bayesian model comparison, leading to the BIC criterion after simplifications.

concepts.name: asymptotic consistency
concepts.description: A property of a model selection criterion whereby the probability of selecting the correct model approaches one as sample size N→∞. BIC is asymptotically consistent while AIC is not.

concepts.name: minimum description length
concepts.description: A model selection approach formally identical to BIC but motivated from optimal coding and data compression theory. The principle states that one should choose the model minimizing the total message length for transmitting both parameters and data.

concepts.name: shannon entropy
concepts.description: A measure of information content in a distribution, equal to -Σᵢ Pr(zᵢ)log₂ Pr(zᵢ) for discrete distributions. Optimal code lengths achieve this lower bound when probabilities satisfy pᵢ = A^(-lᵢ).

concepts.name: huffman coding
concepts.description: A practical coding procedure that can get close to the Shannon entropy lower bound for average message length.

concepts.name: leave-one-out bootstrap
concepts.description: A bootstrap estimation method that only uses predictions from bootstrap samples not containing each observation, avoiding the overlap bias of naive bootstrap.

concepts.name: nonparametric bootstrap
concepts.description:
The bootstrap method described above, in which we sample with re-
placement from the training data, is called the nonparametric bootstrap.
This really means that the method is “model-free,” since it uses the raw
data, not a speciﬁc parametric model, to generate new datasets. Consider
a variation of the bootstrap, called the parametric bootstrap, in which we
simulate new responses by adding Gaussian noise to the predicted values:

concepts.name: log-likelihood
concepts.description:
which we will sometimes abbreviate as ℓ(θ). This expression is called the
log-likelihood, and each value ℓ(θ; zi) = log gθ(zi) is called a log-likelihood
component. The method of maximum likelihood chooses the value θ = ˆθ
to maximize ℓ(θ; Z).
The likelihood function can be used to assess the precision of ˆθ. We need
a few more deﬁnitions. The score function is deﬁned by

concepts.name: data augmentation
concepts.description:
The above procedure is an example of the EM (or Baum–Welch) algorithm
for maximizing likelihoods in certain classes of problems. These problems
are ones for which maximization of the likelihood is diﬃcult, but made
easier by enlarging the sample with latent (unobserved) data. This is called
data augmentation. Here the latent data are the model memberships Δi.
In other problems, the latent data are actual data that should have been
observed but are missing.
Algorithm 8.2 gives the general formulation of the EM algorithm. Our
observed data is Z, having log-likelihood ℓ(θ; Z) depending on parameters
θ. The latent or missing data is Zm, so that the complete data is T =
(Z, Zm) with log-likelihood ℓ0(θ; T), ℓ0 based on the complete density. In
the mixture problem (Z, Zm) = (y, Δ), and ℓ0(θ; T) is given in (8.40).
In our mixture example, E(ℓ0(θ′; T)|Z, ˆθ(j)) is simply (8.40) with the Δi
replaced by the responsibilities ˆγi(ˆθ), and the maximizers in step 3 are just
weighted means and variances.
We now give an explanation of why the EM algorithm works in general.
Since

concepts.name: GEM (generalized EM) algorithms
concepts.description:
Hence the EM iteration never decreases the log-likelihood.
This argument also makes it clear that a full maximization in the M
step is not necessary: we need only to ﬁnd a value ˆθ(j+1) so that Q(θ′, ˆθ(j))
increases as a function of the ﬁrst argument, that is, Q(ˆθ(j+1), ˆθ(j)) >
Q(ˆθ(j), ˆθ(j)). Such procedures are called GEM (generalized EM) algorithms.
The EM algorithm can also be viewed as a minorization procedure: see
Exercise 8.7.

concepts.name: MM algorithms
concepts.description:
There are analogous deﬁnitions for majorization, for minimizing a function
f(x). The resulting algorithms are known as MM algorithms, for “Minorize-
Maximize” or “Majorize-Minimize.”
Show that the EM algorithm (Section 8.5.2) is an example of an MM al-
gorithm, using Q(θ′, θ)+log Pr(Z|θ)−Q(θ, θ) to minorize the observed data
log-likelihood ℓ(θ′; Z). (Note that only the ﬁrst term involves the relevant
parameter θ′).

concepts.name: posterior distribution
concepts.description: In Bayesian inference, the updated distribution of parameters after observing data, computed via Bayes rule from the prior and likelihood.

concepts.name: working response
concepts.description: In IRLS algorithms, an adjusted response variable computed at each iteration for fitting weighted regression.

concepts.name: .632 estimator
concepts.description: A bootstrap estimate of prediction error designed to alleviate the upward bias of leave-one-out bootstrap by pulling it toward the training error rate using the constant .632.

concepts.name: .632+ estimator
concepts.description: An improved version of the .632 estimator that accounts for overfitting by adjusting the weight between leave-one-out bootstrap and training error based on the relative overfitting rate.

concepts.name: no-information error rate
concepts.description: The error rate of a prediction rule if the inputs and class labels were independent, used to assess the amount of overfitting in bootstrap estimates.

concepts.name: relative overfitting rate
concepts.description: A quantity measuring the degree of overfitting, ranging from 0 (no overfitting) to 1 (overfitting equals the no-information value), used in the .632+ estimator.

concepts.name: parametric bootstrap
concepts.description: A bootstrap method that simulates new responses by adding Gaussian noise to predicted values, rather than sampling from raw data.

concepts.name: likelihood function
concepts.description: The probability of the observed data under a parametric model, defined as the product of densities over all observations.

concepts.name: score function
concepts.description: The derivative of the log-likelihood with respect to parameters, which equals zero at the maximum likelihood estimate.

concepts.name: information matrix
concepts.description: The negative second derivative matrix of the log-likelihood, used to assess precision of maximum likelihood estimates.

concepts.name: observed information
concepts.description: The information matrix evaluated at the maximum likelihood estimate.

concepts.name: fisher information
concepts.description: The expected value of the information matrix, also called expected information.

concepts.name: noninformative prior
concepts.description: A constant or very diffuse prior distribution that has minimal influence on the posterior, allowing data to dominate inference.

concepts.name: predictive distribution
concepts.description: The Bayesian distribution for future observations, integrating over the posterior distribution of parameters.

concepts.name: gaussian process prior
concepts.description: A prior distribution on functions specified by defining the covariance between any two function values.

concepts.name: covariance kernel
concepts.description: A function defining the covariance structure of a Gaussian process prior.

concepts.name: expectation-maximization algorithm
concepts.description: An iterative algorithm for maximum likelihood estimation in problems with latent variables, alternating between computing expected values of latent data (E-step) and maximizing likelihood (M-step).

concepts.name: latent data
concepts.description: Unobserved or missing data that, if known, would simplify likelihood maximization in the EM algorithm.

concepts.name: complete data log-likelihood
concepts.description: The log-likelihood based on both observed and latent data, used in the EM algorithm framework.

concepts.name: generalized em algorithms
concepts.description: Variants of EM that only require increasing (not maximizing) the Q function in the M-step.

concepts.name: markov chain monte carlo
concepts.description: A class of algorithms for sampling from probability distributions by constructing a Markov chain with the desired distribution as its stationary distribution.

concepts.name: gibbs sampling
concepts.description: An MCMC method that generates samples from a joint distribution by iteratively sampling from conditional distributions of each variable given the others.

concepts.name: stationary distribution
concepts.description: In MCMC, the limiting distribution of the Markov chain, which is the target distribution from which we wish to sample.

concepts.name: burn-in period
concepts.description: The initial iterations of MCMC that are discarded before the chain reaches stationarity.

concepts.name: bagging
concepts.description: Bootstrap aggregation: a method that averages predictions over bootstrap samples to reduce variance and improve prediction accuracy.

concepts.name: empirical distribution
concepts.description: A distribution putting equal probability on each observed data point, used as the sampling distribution in nonparametric bootstrap.

concepts.name: consensus vote
concepts.description: In bagging for classification, combining predictions by majority voting across bootstrap samples.

concepts.name: weak learners
concepts.description: Classifiers with error rate better than random but not necessarily strong, which can be combined to form a strong classifier.

concepts.name: bayesian model averaging
concepts.description: A Bayesian approach to combining predictions from multiple models, weighting each by its posterior probability.

concepts.name: committee methods
concepts.description: Model averaging strategies that take a simple unweighted average of predictions from multiple models.

concepts.name: stacking
concepts.description: Stacked generalization: a model averaging method that uses cross-validated predictions to learn optimal weights for combining models, accounting for model complexity.

concepts.name: bumping
concepts.description: Bootstrap-based model improvement that fits models to multiple bootstrap samples and selects the one with best training set performance, helping escape poor local minima.

concepts.name: link function
concepts.description: A function relating the conditional mean of a response to an additive function of predictors in generalized models.

concepts.name: backfitting algorithm
concepts.description: An iterative procedure for fitting additive models by cycling through predictors, applying a smoother to partial residuals for each predictor while holding others fixed.

concepts.name: pasting
concepts.description:
ent as possible. The splitting rules deﬁning the boxes are related to each
through a binary tree, facilitating their interpretation.
The patient rule induction method (PRIM) also ﬁnds boxes in the feature
space, but seeks boxes in which the response average is high. Hence it looks
for maxima in the target function, an exercise known as bump hunting. (If
minima rather than maxima are desired, one simply works with the negative
response values.)
PRIM also diﬀers from tree-based partitioning methods in that the box
deﬁnitions are not described by a binary tree. This makes interpretation of
the collection of rules more diﬃcult; however, by removing the binary tree
constraint, the individual rules are often simpler.
The main box construction method in PRIM works from the top down,
starting with a box containing all of the data. The box is compressed along
one face by a small amount, and the observations then falling outside the
box are peeled oﬀ. The face chosen for compression is the one resulting in
the largest box mean, after the compression is performed. Then the process
is repeated, stopping when the current box contains some minimum number
of data points.
This process is illustrated in Figure 9.7. There are 200 data points uni-
formly distributed over the unit square. The color-coded plot indicates the
response Y taking the value 1 (red) when 0.5 < X1 < 0.8 and 0.4 < X2 <
0.6. and zero (blue) otherwise. The panels shows the successive boxes found
by the top-down peeling procedure, peeling oﬀa proportion α = 0.1 of the
remaining data points at each stage.
Figure 9.8 shows the mean of the response values in the box, as the box
is compressed.
After the top-down sequence is computed, PRIM reverses the process,
expanding along any edge, if such an expansion increases the box mean.
This is called pasting. Since the top-down procedure is greedy at each step,
such an expansion is often possible.
The result of these steps is a sequence of boxes, with diﬀerent numbers
of observation in each box. Cross-validation, combined with the judgment
of the data analyst, is used to choose the optimal box size.
Denote by B1 the indices of the observations in the box found in step 1.
The PRIM procedure then removes the observations in B1 from the training
set, and the two-step process—top down peeling, followed by bottom-up
pasting—is repeated on the remaining dataset. This entire process is re-
peated several times, producing a sequence of boxes B1, B2, . . . , Bk. Each
box is deﬁned by a set of rules involving a subset of predictors like

concepts.name: bump hunting
concepts.description:
ent as possible. The splitting rules deﬁning the boxes are related to each
through a binary tree, facilitating their interpretation.
The patient rule induction method (PRIM) also ﬁnds boxes in the feature
space, but seeks boxes in which the response average is high. Hence it looks
for maxima in the target function, an exercise known as bump hunting. (If
minima rather than maxima are desired, one simply works with the negative
response values.)
PRIM also diﬀers from tree-based partitioning methods in that the box
deﬁnitions are not described by a binary tree. This makes interpretation of
the collection of rules more diﬃcult; however, by removing the binary tree
constraint, the individual rules are often simpler.
The main box construction method in PRIM works from the top down,
starting with a box containing all of the data. The box is compressed along
one face by a small amount, and the observations then falling outside the
box are peeled oﬀ. The face chosen for compression is the one resulting in
the largest box mean, after the compression is performed. Then the process
is repeated, stopping when the current box contains some minimum number
of data points.
This process is illustrated in Figure 9.7. There are 200 data points uni-
formly distributed over the unit square. The color-coded plot indicates the
response Y taking the value 1 (red) when 0.5 < X1 < 0.8 and 0.4 < X2 <
0.6. and zero (blue) otherwise. The panels shows the successive boxes found
by the top-down peeling procedure, peeling oﬀa proportion α = 0.1 of the
remaining data points at each stage.
Figure 9.8 shows the mean of the response values in the box, as the box
is compressed.
After the top-down sequence is computed, PRIM reverses the process,
expanding along any edge, if such an expansion increases the box mean.
This is called pasting. Since the top-down procedure is greedy at each step,
such an expansion is often possible.
The result of these steps is a sequence of boxes, with diﬀerent numbers
of observation in each box. Cross-validation, combined with the judgment
of the data analyst, is used to choose the optimal box size.
Denote by B1 the indices of the observations in the box found in step 1.
The PRIM procedure then removes the observations in B1 from the training
set, and the two-step process—top down peeling, followed by bottom-up
pasting—is repeated on the remaining dataset. This entire process is re-
peated several times, producing a sequence of boxes B1, B2, . . . , Bk. Each
box is deﬁned by a set of rules involving a subset of predictors like

concepts.name: gating networks
concepts.description:
The hierarchical mixtures of experts (HME) procedure can be viewed as a
variant of tree-based methods. The main diﬀerence is that the tree splits
are not hard decisions but rather soft probabilistic ones. At each node an
observation goes left or right with probabilities depending on its input val-
ues. This has some computational advantages since the resulting parameter
optimization problem is smooth, unlike the discrete split point search in the
tree-based approach. The soft splits might also help in prediction accuracy
and provide a useful alternative description of the data.
There are other diﬀerences between HMEs and the CART implementa-
tion of trees. In an HME, a linear (or logistic regression) model is ﬁt in
each terminal node, instead of a constant as in CART. The splits can be
multiway, not just binary, and the splits are probabilistic functions of a
linear combination of inputs, rather than a single input as in the standard
use of CART. However, the relative merits of these choices are not clear,
and most were discussed at the end of Section 9.2.
A simple two-level HME model in shown in Figure 9.13. It can be thought
of as a tree with soft splits at each non-terminal node. However, the inven-
tors of this methodology use a diﬀerent terminology. The terminal nodes
are called experts, and the non-terminal nodes are called gating networks.
The idea is that each expert provides an opinion (prediction) about the
response, and these are combined together by the gating networks. As we
will see, the model is formally a mixture model, and the two-level model
in the ﬁgure can be extend to multiple levels, hence the name hierarchical
mixtures of experts.

concepts.name: margin in classification
concepts.description: The product yf(x) in classification with y ∈ {-1,1}. Positive margin indicates correct classification, negative margin indicates misclassification. Analogous to residuals in regression.

concepts.name: generalized additive model
concepts.description: A regression model where the response is related to predictors through a sum of smooth nonparametric functions, extending linear models to allow flexible nonlinear effects.

concepts.name: additive logistic regression
concepts.description: A generalized additive model for binary outcomes using the logit link, replacing linear terms with smooth functions.

concepts.name: identity link
concepts.description: The link function g(μ) = μ, used for linear and additive models with Gaussian responses.

concepts.name: logit link
concepts.description: The link function g(μ) = log[μ/(1−μ)] for modeling binomial probabilities.

concepts.name: probit link
concepts.description: The inverse Gaussian CDF as a link function for modeling binomial probabilities.

concepts.name: log link
concepts.description: The link function g(μ) = log(μ) for log-linear or log-additive models with Poisson count data.

concepts.name: local scoring algorithm
concepts.description: An iteratively reweighted least squares algorithm for fitting generalized additive models, combining backfitting with Newton-Raphson optimization.

concepts.name: recursive binary partition
concepts.description: A tree-building strategy that splits feature space into regions by recursively splitting into two subregions at each step, using axis-parallel splits.

concepts.name: cost-complexity pruning
concepts.description: A tree pruning method that balances tree size against goodness of fit by minimizing a penalized criterion Cα(T) = Σ NmQm(T) + α|T|.

concepts.name: weakest link pruning
concepts.description: A method to find the optimal subtree Tα by successively collapsing internal nodes that produce the smallest per-node increase in node impurity.

concepts.name: node impurity
concepts.description: A measure of heterogeneity within a tree node, used to guide splitting decisions. For classification, common measures are Gini index, cross-entropy, and misclassification error.

concepts.name: gini index
concepts.description: A node impurity measure for classification trees equal to Σk≠k' p̂mk p̂mk' = ΣK p̂mk(1 - p̂mk), measuring the probability of misclassification if class labels are randomly assigned according to node proportions.

concepts.name: cross-entropy for trees
concepts.description: A node impurity measure for classification trees defined as -ΣK p̂mk log p̂mk, which is more sensitive to changes in node probabilities than misclassification rate.

concepts.name: surrogate splits
concepts.description: Alternative splitting predictors used in tree construction when the primary splitting predictor has missing values, exploiting correlations between predictors.

concepts.name: sensitivity in classification
concepts.description: The probability of predicting disease (positive class) given the true state is disease; also called true positive rate.

concepts.name: specificity in classification
concepts.description: The probability of predicting non-disease (negative class) given the true state is non-disease; also called true negative rate.

concepts.name: receiver operating characteristic curve
concepts.description: A plot of sensitivity versus specificity as classification threshold varies, used to assess the tradeoff between true positive and false positive rates. Area under ROC curve summarizes classifier performance.

concepts.name: c-statistic
concepts.description: The area under the ROC curve, equivalent to the Mann-Whitney U statistic for the median difference between prediction scores in two groups.

concepts.name: patient rule induction method
concepts.description:
2. Consider shrinking the box by compressing one face, so as to peel oﬀ
the proportion α of observations having either the highest values of
a predictor Xj, or the lowest. Choose the peeling that produces the
highest response mean in the remaining box. (Typically α = 0.05 or
0.10.)

concepts.name: top-down peeling
concepts.description: PRIM's greedy algorithm that starts with a box containing all data, then repeatedly compresses one face to peel off observations, choosing the compression that maximizes the box mean.

concepts.name: mars basis functions
concepts.description: Piecewise linear functions (x-t)+ and (t-x)+ used in MARS, forming reflected pairs with knots at observed values. Products of these create tensor products for modeling interactions.

concepts.name: reflected pair
concepts.description: In MARS, the two complementary basis functions (x-t)+ and (t-x)+ that form a knot at value t, allowing locally adaptive modeling.

concepts.name: generalized cross-validation for mars
concepts.description: A criterion GCV(λ) = Σ(yi - f̂λ(xi))²/N / [1 - M(λ)/N]² used to select model size in MARS, accounting for effective parameters including knot selection penalty.

concepts.name: hierarchical mixtures of experts
concepts.description: A tree-based method with soft probabilistic splits instead of hard decisions, fitting linear or logistic regression models in terminal nodes (experts) combined by gating networks.

concepts.name: gating networks in hme
concepts.description: Soft multinomial logistic functions gj(x,γj) = e^(γjᵀx) / Σ e^(γkᵀx) at internal nodes of HME that assign observations to branches probabilistically.

concepts.name: missing at random
concepts.description: Data are MAR if the distribution of the missingness indicator R depends on the data Z only through observed entries Zobs, not on unobserved values.

concepts.name: missing completely at random
concepts.description: Data are MCAR if the distribution of missingness doesn't depend on observed or missing data: Pr(R|Z,θ) = Pr(R|θ). Stronger assumption than MAR.

concepts.name: weak classifier
concepts.description: A classifier whose error rate is only slightly better than random guessing. Boosting combines many weak classifiers to produce a powerful committee.

concepts.name: multinomial deviance
concepts.description: Extension of binomial deviance to K classes: -Σ I(yi=Gk) log pk(xi) where pk(x) = e^fk(x)/Σe^fℓ(x). Penalizes incorrect predictions linearly.
